html:
Kesla Oyj A, KELAS.HE

Kesla is no longer just a weak Finnish forestry equipment micro-cap. It is a stressed industrial turnaround with a real defense option, and that distinction matters. At the May 15 close of €5.70, the equity value is roughly €19.3 million using 3.383 million A and B shares, and enterprise value is about €29 million using Q1 net debt of €9.7 million.

The market has reasons to distrust it. 2025 was ugly: revenue fell 22% to €34.4 million, EBIT was -€4.9 million, EPS was -€1.32, liquidity was tight, covenants were breached, and the company ended the year with only €0.5 million of cash and available credit lines. That is why the stock had traded like a financing-risk machine shop.

The variant perception is that the financing stress is not the core story anymore. It is the setup. Kesla’s May 7 Q1 report showed orders of €27.8 million, up 141%, backlog of €27.5 million, up 168%, and about €16.8 million of that backlog from KESLA Defence. The March NATO-country Kerberos order is worth about €17 million, starts delivery in early summer 2026, runs into spring 2027, includes spare parts, service systems and training, and is not just a one-off catalog sale. Kerberos units are already in active or test use in three countries, and Kesla says international sales work has been underway for nearly two years.

That changes the underwriting. A €17 million order is almost 90% of today’s market cap and about half of 2025 revenue. More importantly, it lands in the exact place where Kesla has been broken: factory utilization. Management explicitly said the order materially improves plant utilization and creates a basis for profitability improvement. This is an operating leverage story before it is a multiple story.

The second misunderstood piece is that Kesla is actively shrinking away from the wrong business. On May 11, the board started a divestment process for the civilian Tractor Equipment product group, while retaining rights tied to defense and non-tractor-mounted applications. That is not cosmetic. It is a capital allocation signal: management is willing to sell or restructure a legacy civilian line to fund and focus the defense opportunity. In a micro-cap, this kind of simplification can matter more than a slide-deck strategy reset.

The third piece is that the bridge financing reduces the immediate “death spiral” risk. Kesla negotiated a Nordic bank bridge facility with two €2.5 million tranches, plus receivables financing. The facility is short term and has to be repaid by year-end 2026, so it is not free money. But paired with the NATO order, it turns the question from “can Kesla survive the next few quarters?” into “can Kesla execute enough backlog to refinance from a position of improving earnings?”

The current guidance is deliberately unexciting: 2026 revenue of €38-45 million and EBIT margin between -2% and +2%, assuming stable conditions. That should not be confused with normalized earnings power. Only about €7 million of the NATO order is expected in 2026, with roughly €10 million in 2027. The Q1 revenue base still reflected the weak 2025 order book. The real earnings test is H2 2026 through 2027, when defense deliveries, receivables financing, plant utilization, and the tractor divestment all hit the model.

Base case: Kesla exits 2026 at roughly break-even EBIT, then reaches €45-50 million of 2027 revenue as the remaining NATO order is delivered and civilian cranes/logging equipment stabilize. At a 5-6% EBIT margin, which is not aggressive for a better-utilized niche industrial, EBIT is €2.3-3.0 million. After interest, tax shields, and normalized working capital, earnings power can be €1.6-2.2 million, or €0.47-0.65 per share. A defense-validated Finnish industrial supplier with improving governance and a cleaner portfolio should not trade at 8x forward earnings. Even 14x €0.60 supports about €8.40, and modest debt reduction or follow-on orders pushes the base case toward €9-10.

The bull case is not “the existing order earns a high margin.” The bull case is that the existing order proves procurement qualification. A NATO-country order with confidential unit count, location, and use case is a reference customer. If Kerberos becomes a recurring modular platform for engineering, logistics, mobility and countermobility in forested or off-road environments, Kesla’s defense revenue can become a €20-25 million annual stream, not a €17 million project. At that point group revenue can be €55-60 million, EBIT margin can move toward 8%, and net income can approach €3.5-4.0 million. A 12-15x multiple on that is €42-60 million of equity value before giving much credit for strategic scarcity.

There is also a governance catalyst hiding in plain sight. Kesla has an A/B share structure, with B shares carrying 20 votes, and at Q1 board members held only 15.5% of shares but 50.8% of votes. The company is now reviewing a possible share-class merger. This may not happen, and terms matter, but if it does, the discount for control, liquidity and complexity should narrow.

The fire at the Joensuu factory looks over-weighted in earlier versions of the thesis. The company described it as a smoldering fire in a specific roof penetration area, extinguished by rescue authorities after opening roof structures. The later Q1 release still guided defense deliveries to begin in summer 2026, and Kesla separately announced it was recruiting about 10 permanent employees at Kesälahti and ending furloughs because of stronger production volumes and backlog. The fire is not the main risk unless new disclosures show hidden damage.

The real risks are financing and execution. Q1 net gearing was 147.5%, equity ratio 25.5%, and the company needs H1 2026 EBITDA of at least €0.1 million under the revised covenant path before original year-end covenant levels return. The bridge loan is short term. A dilutive equity raise is possible, especially since the AGM authorized up to 338,313 A shares, about 10% of share count. The R&D head resignation also matters for a company whose bull case depends on product adaptation and defense applications.

Retail and social context is now a tailwind, but not yet a mania. The shareholder count rose to 3,162 at Q1, the stock traded 588,586 shares in Q1 versus 339,057 a year ago, and the mid-May price action showed much heavier daily volume than the old micro-cap baseline. Finnish retail platforms are now grouping Kesla alongside defense-adjacent names such as Summa Defence and Bittium. That attention can re-rate the stock quickly because the float is small, but it also means volatility is now part of the trade.

The cleanest way to frame Kesla is this: at €5.70, the market is no longer giving away the NATO order for free, but it is still pricing Kesla like the order is a temporary rescue rather than the first proof point in a defense transition. The downside is a financing stumble. The upside is a repaired balance sheet, a divested low-return product group, a qualified NATO reference, and a 2027 earnings base that has little relationship to 2025 trailing losses.

Bear target: €3.00, down 47%. Defense execution is low margin or delayed, civilian demand stays weak, covenant pressure returns, and equity dilution absorbs much of the order value.

Base target: €9.50, up 67%. Kesla delivers the NATO order, avoids severe dilution, divests or restructures Tractor Equipment, and reaches €2 million plus normalized net income in 2027.

Bull target: €16.50, up 189%. Kerberos wins follow-on NATO orders, defense becomes a recurring €20 million plus revenue stream, EBIT margins move toward high single digits, and the equity re-rates from distressed micro-cap to scarce Nordic defense supplier.
